inspect-unstaged-changes-with-diff.label: Add and Inspect Exact Project Changes
inspect-unstaged-changes-with-diff.summary: Add one third file, then inspect two supplied text edits without staging them.

sketch2.title: Name the File When Focus Matters

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.title: Create the Third Project File
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.prompt:
The community project continues with the two tracked files from Module 1: `README.md` and `schedule.txt`. The community organizers now need a contact list, but `contacts.txt` does not exist yet.

Create `community-project/contacts.txt`. Starting on line 1, enter exactly:

```text
Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0100
```
Then use the terminal to:

1. Inspect the short status and confirm that Git shows `?? contacts.txt`.
2. Stage only `contacts.txt`.
3. Create a focused commit with the exact message `Add community contacts`.

When you finish, the repository should contain three tracked files and two commits. Do not change `README.md` or `schedule.txt`.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.hint: Create and save the exact three-line file first. Then inspect it as untracked, stage only that file, and commit it with the supplied message.

help.concept: An untracked file is not part of history until it is staged and committed. `git status --short` identifies it with `??`; `git add contacts.txt` stages it; `git commit -m "Add community contacts"` records it as the repository’s third file.
help.hint_2: The new commit should add only `contacts.txt`.

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.solutionCode:
git status --short
git add contacts.txt
git commit -m "Add community contacts"

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Start community project'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.instructions:
`community-project/` should begin this lesson with only `README.md` and `schedule.txt`; `contacts.txt` should not exist yet. Create `community-project/contacts.txt`. Starting on line 1, enter exactly these three lines: `Community contacts`, a blank line, and `Help desk: 555-0100`. Save the file.

Then use the terminal to inspect short status, stage only `contacts.txt`, and record it with the exact commit message `Add community contacts`.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you inspect the new file before staging it?

2.message: Did you record the contact list as its own commit?

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.title: Change and Inspect the Cleanup Time
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.prompt:
The cleanup coordinator moved Saturday’s start time earlier so volunteers can finish before an afternoon event. Open `community-project/schedule.txt` and find this line:

`Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM`

Replace it with:

`Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM`

Save the file. Then use the command you learned earlier to inspect only the unstaged changes in `schedule.txt`.

Do not stage the file yet.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.hint: Make the exact one-line edit, then compare the working copy with the staged version.

help.concept: `git diff -- schedule.txt` shows unstaged line changes for that file. A removed line begins with `-` and an added line begins with `+`. The command is read-only.
help.hint_2: The diff should show one removed line and one added line.

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.title: Change and Inspect the Help Desk Number
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.prompt:
The help desk has a new phone number, so the project contact list must be corrected. Open `community-project/contacts.txt` and find this line:

`Help desk: 555-0100`

Replace it with:

`Help desk: 555-0130`. Then use the command you learned earlier to inspect only the unstaged changes in `contacts.txt`.

Leave the changes in both `schedule.txt` and `contacts.txt` unstaged.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.hint: Make the exact contact edit and narrow the diff to that path.

help.concept: `git diff -- contacts.txt` limits the unstaged comparison to the contact file even though another file is also modified. Naming the path changes only the output, not repository state.
help.hint_2: The schedule change must remain present for the next topic.

single-choice-1.title: Using git diff
single-choice-1.hint: Ordinary `git diff` is most useful while a working-tree edit is still unstaged.

help.concept: Move from a file-level status report to a line-level review, and separate unrelated edits before they reach the staging area.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: After the change has already been committed
options.c: When you want to list earlier commit subjects
options.d: When you want to create repository metadata

multi-choice-2.title: Interpreting Diff Output
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which symbol in `git diff` output indicates a line has been added?
multi-choice-2.hint: Choose the one answer that describes this command or concept precisely.

help.concept: Move from a file-level status report to a line-level review, and separate unrelated edits before they reach the staging area.
help.hint_1: Remove choices that act on a different repository layer or promise behavior Git does not provide.
help.hint_2: Prefer the exact definition used in this chapter over a nearby Git idea.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Symbol for Removed Lines
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: In `git diff`, lines that have been removed are indicated by the symbol `[blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Move from a file-level status report to a line-level review, and separate unrelated edits before they reach the staging area.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

fill-blank-choice-1.template: In `git diff`, lines that have been removed are indicated by the symbol `[blank1]`.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Purpose of git diff
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: The primary purpose of `git diff` is to show changes in the [blank1] that are not yet staged.
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

sketch0.title: Add One File for Module 2
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Congratulations on completing Module 1. You now know how to inspect a project, initialize a repository, configure a local commit identity, stage files, create a commit, and verify the result.

In Module 2, you will learn new commands and continue practicing the commands from Module 1 in a more realistic workflow.

sketch2.title: Read an Exact Text Change
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
The command `git diff -- schedule.txt` compares the working copy of `schedule.txt` with the version currently in the staging area.

In the output:

* Lines beginning with `-` were removed.
* Lines beginning with `+` were added.

The command is read-only. It does not stage, discard, or modify any changes, so it is safe to use before deciding what to stage.

sketch3.title: Narrow a Diff to One File
sketch3.bodyMarkdown: Continue practicing with the helpful `git diff` command.
